item.title: Screams Echo as Venezuela Searches for Survivors After Quakes
item.description: Screams and groans echoed from beneath piles of concrete and bricks during the early morning hours on Thursday as residents searched for survivors in the coastal city of Catia La Mar, where rescue teams had yet to reach several collapsed buildings nearly 10 hours after twin earthquakes struck Venezuela.

item.title: Hedge Fund Eyes Crop Disruption as Super El Niño Looms
item.description: Moreton Capital Partners is shifting focus to extreme weather.

item.title: Why the Trump Administration Is Defending Musk’s xAI Data Centers in Court
item.description: The government’s bid to shield the AI company from a pollution lawsuit is raising concerns that citizens could lose a longstanding tool for enforcing environmental rules.

item.title: What Americans Who Worry About Climate Change Are Getting Wrong
item.description: New polling suggests people in the US and other wealthy countries underrate others’ anxiety about global warming.

item.title: TotalEnergies Told It Can’t Just Ignore Its Clients’ Emissions
item.description: TotalEnergies SE was ordered by a Paris court to amend its climate change plans to also target the impact of indirect emissions produced in its value chain by customers.

item.title: In ‘Modern-Day Noah’s Ark,’ Startup Colossal Will Bank Samples of At-Risk US Species
item.description: The company is working with the federal government on a vault that will store frozen material from plants and animals protected by the Endangered Species Act.

item.title: EU Seeks Stronger Liquidity Safeguards in Carbon Reform
item.description: The European Union plans to make its flagship carbon market more resilient by introducing dynamic controls on the supply of emission permits, with the aim of preserving liquidity and preventing price spikes.

item.title: London Faces Huge Financial Cost Tied to Rising Heat, Mayor Says
item.description: London’s efforts to deal with rising temperatures come at a considerable financial cost that will require the city to turn to private investors for help, the mayor of the UK capital said.

item.title: Weather Models Show Swath of US Turning Warmer by End of June
item.description: Overnight weather models point to a warmer-than-expected end of June and beginning of July, boosting cooling demand, according to the Commodity Weather Group LLC

item.title: Iran Conflict Accelerating Shift to Renewables, John Kerry Says
item.description: The Iran war has changed perceptions of energy markets, according to former US secretary of state John Kerry, with oil and gas viewed as vulnerable to trade choke points while sources like solar and wind gain ground.

item.title: Vietnam to Start Carbon Market Emissions Trading Next Week
item.description: Vietnam will begin its pilot carbon market next week with 92 local companies, including Hoa Phat Group, Formosa Ha Tinh Steel Corp. and Vietnam Electricity Group, trading emissions quotas.

item.title: CME Plans to Launch Wind Derivatives for US, Europe, Australia
item.description: The Chicago Mercantile Exchange plans to launch wind derivatives across three continents, according to a person familiar with the matter, as the role of renewable energy in power generation grows.

item.title: Heat Wave Breaks French and UK Records as Temperatures Soar
item.description: The heat wave baking western Europe smashed records in the UK and France, as the region faces another day of disruptive temperatures.

item.title: Top Climate Fund Says 75 Countries Apply for Adaptation Finance
item.description: The Climate Investment Funds, a $14 billion pool of climate-focused capital, said 75 countries applied for adaptation finance through a new program designed to promote initiatives aimed at providing protection against the impact of climate warming.

item.title: US Solar Is Thriving in the Face of Trump’s Tariffs
item.description: The American administration is dismantling clean-energy policies, yet 90% of all new power-generating capacity added to the grid last year was made up of solar, wind and batteries.

item.title: India’s $55 Billion Green Energy Pipeline Faces Climate Damage
item.description: The majority of India’s planned renewable energy infrastructure will be exposed to escalating climate hazards, putting about $55 billion of physical assets at risk of damage by the end of this decade, according to a new study.

item.title: Guangzhou Exchange Considering Potential of Cobalt Metal Futures
item.description: Guangzhou Futures Exchange is considering launching China’s first futures for cobalt, a metal used in batteries as well as the aerospace and defense industries, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: EU’s Methane Rules Will Drive Away US Gas Supplies, Wright Says
item.description: US Energy Secretary Chris Wright warned the European Union that US gas supplies will head elsewhere if the bloc refuses to ease regulations to tackle methane, a potent greenhouse gas.

item.title: US Largest Grid Updates Emergency Plan as AI Stretches Capacity
item.description: The largest US power grid is creating a new emergency warning as surging data-center demand pushes electricity supplies toward shortages beyond periods of extreme weather.

item.title: Modern Homes Leave Londoners Struggling to Cope With Extreme Heat
item.description: The British capital is baking as a heat wave sweeps through Europe.

item.title: More Americans Are Moving Away From Flood Risk Than Toward It
item.description: Florida’s Miami-Dade and other flood-prone US counties have lost residents, suggesting that climate risk and its costs are influencing some relocation decisions.

item.title: Unseasonal Heat for Great Lakes While Western US on Fire Watch
item.description: The Great Lakes and large parts of Ontario and Quebec are forecast to see average temperatures as much as 8F (4C) degrees above normal through June 29 to July 3, according to Commodity Weather Group.

item.title: China’s Electric Vehicle Exports Reach Record High in May
item.description: Countries looking to cut fossil-fuel imports are leaning on China’s cheap electricity technologies

item.title: Slate Tests Electric Pickup Market With Price Just Under $25,000
item.description: Slate Auto, the EV startup backed by Jeff Bezos, said its all-electric subcompact pickup will be priced just under $25,000, a price tag it’s betting can help it take on bigger, more established rivals.

item.title: Heat Is on Central Bankers Setting Rates
item.description: In a sweltering week for Europe, central bankers are getting advice on how to handle climate change.

item.title: AI-Powered Emails Are Influencing Politics on Hot-Button Issues
item.description: From California to North Carolina, officials weighing policy changes are being inundated with comments via platforms that tout AI capabilities.

item.title: China Says Iran Crisis Shows Nations Must Speed Up Energy Shift
item.description: Oil and gas shortages triggered by the US war against Iran should be a lesson to countries to accelerate their energy transitions, according to China’s climate envoy.

item.title: France Records Hottest Day Ever as European Heat Wave Deepens
item.description: France recorded its hottest ever night and day as the heat wave searing western Europe deepened, disrupting schools, transport and tourist sites.

item.title: Africa’s Biggest Solar Mini-Grid Operator Sells Stake to Expand
item.description: WeLight, which operates more solar mini-grids in Africa than any other company, said the International Finance Corp. bought a stake in the business, backing its expansion into Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: Londoners Complain of ‘Unbearable Heat’ in City’s Modern Housing
item.description: In Britain’s capital city, an architectural trend that started about two decades ago is now being linked to unbearable levels of indoor heat.
